captureFilters.description: All traffic to or from a specific host

displayFilters.description: All HTTP requests in the capture
TLS Client Hello (start of TLS sessions)
Match a literal string anywhere in the frame
Show retransmitted packets — congestion or loss

cliTools.name: tshark
cliTools.description: Wireshark's CLI counterpart — same dissectors, no GUI. The right choice for scripting, pipelines, and remote captures.

cliTools.name: dumpcap
cliTools.description: The minimal capture engine Wireshark itself uses. Lower memory footprint and safer for long-running unattended captures.

cliTools.name: editcap
cliTools.description: Edit, split, slice, deduplicate, or convert capture files (e.g. trim a 4-hour pcap down to one suspicious minute).

cliTools.name: mergecap
cliTools.description: Merge multiple captures into one timeline — useful when several sensors recorded the same incident.

cliTools.name: capinfos
cliTools.description: Quick metadata dump for a capture: size, packet count, time range, average packet rate.

cliTools.name: text2pcap
cliTools.description: Convert hex-dump text (from logs or papers) into a .pcap so it can be opened in Wireshark.

useCases.title: Hunting C2 beacons
useCases.details: Beacons show as periodic, regular-interval connections to one or two remote hosts. Use Statistics → IO Graph at a 1s interval to spot the heartbeat, then tcp.stream eq N to drill into a single beacon flow and inspect its payload.

useCases.title: Detecting data exfiltration
useCases.details: Statistics → Conversations, sorted by Bytes. Anomalously large outbound flows to non-business destinations are the prime candidates. Filter ip.dst != <internal-net> and look for steady upload patterns.

useCases.title: DNS tunnelling
useCases.details: Filter dns. Long, high-entropy subdomains, excessive TXT/NULL records, or one client driving thousands of queries to a single resolver are tell-tale signs. Statistics → DNS gives quick aggregates.

useCases.title: Cleartext credential capture
useCases.details: http.request.method == "POST" or ftp.request.command == "PASS" or telnet. Right-click → Follow → HTTP/TCP Stream to reconstruct the full exchange including form-encoded credentials.

useCases.title: Lateral movement over SMB
useCases.details: smb2 with focus on tree-connect requests to admin shares (ADMIN$, C$, IPC$). Cross-reference Kerberos AS-REQ/TGS-REQ to map who authenticated where and when.

useCases.title: Reconstructing transferred files
useCases.details: File → Export Objects → HTTP / SMB / FTP / TFTP / IMF reassembles complete payloads (executables, documents, attachments) for offline triage in your sandbox or static analysis tooling.

stats.purpose: Top talkers per protocol — sorted by bytes, packets, duration.
Per-host summary including GeoIP if MaxMind databases are configured.
Traffic mix by protocol — instant view of what's flowing.
Throughput over time. Layer multiple filters to compare flows visually.
Sequence-diagram view of a conversation — great for handshake debugging.
Auto-collated warnings, retransmits, malformed packets, and protocol anomalies.

tips: Coloring rules (View → Coloring Rules) make protocol problems visible at a glance — black means TCP errors, red means a serious problem.
Right-click → "Apply as Filter" / "Prepare a Filter" beats memorising syntax — Wireshark generates the right expression for you.
Tools → Lua Console lets you script custom dissectors for proprietary protocols.
Use Edit → Preferences → Name Resolution sparingly — DNS lookups against capture data can leak the analyst's IP and slow Wireshark to a crawl.
Capture to ring-buffer files (-b filesize:100 -b files:10) for long captures so disk doesn't fill and you keep the most recent window.
On Linux, capture as an unprivileged user via setcap'd dumpcap — never run the GUI as root.
limitations: TLS-encrypted payloads are opaque without the session keys (SSLKEYLOGFILE) or a server private key (RSA only, not DHE).
Capture is only as good as the tap point — switched networks need port mirroring (SPAN) or a TAP, not just plugging into a wall jack.
Wire-rate capture on 10G+ links needs specialised hardware (Napatech, Endace, AF_PACKET v3) — software-only Wireshark will drop packets.
Decoding is dissector-bound — proprietary or new protocols may show as raw bytes until a Lua dissector is written.
Encrypted protocols (QUIC, DoH, ECH) are increasingly the norm and erode visibility — plan for endpoint telemetry, not just packet capture.
